text:
Problem Overview

In this problem, students extend their work with equivalent ratios by making tables of equivalent ratios. Students then use a rate table to find many equivalent comparisons. The table can be used to find unit rates, and the unit rates can be used to find other equivalent ratios.

A rate is a special kind of ratio. A rate is always a comparison of two different units, e.g, miles per hour, dollars per gallon, etc. Common Core does not introduce the vocabulary of rate to students until grade 7. However, unit rate is a grade 6 Common Core concept. A unit rate is a ratio or tae where one of the quantities is 1.

Arc of Learning: Exploration, Analysis

Standards: See your state alignment chart.

NWDYK

How are rate tables and unit rates and equations useful in solving problems?

Emerging Mathematical Ideas

Begin recognizing and making sense of unit rates in various representations and explore using them to solve ratio problems.

Notice that

  • one of the quantities in a unit rate comparison is 1, y: 1 or 1: x;
  • unit rates are useful in solving problems with ratios because they allow you to easily calculate any quantity in a ratio given the other quantity; and
  • unit rates are useful in building or completing a rate table and in finding other equivalent ratios.

Make connections between unit rates and equations relating the two quantities and a graph of the relationship between the two quantities.

Launch (Getting Started)

Connecting to Prior Knowledge

Revisit Problem 2.2: Selling Cupcakes. Remind students that they found the cost for 1 box of cupcakes. they found the unit rate for the price of 1 box. Introduce the vocabulary of unit rate and rate tables.

Suggested Questions

  • Have you experienced a situation in which finding 1 of something was helpful?
  • We found the cost of 1 large box of cupcakes was $12. Can you think of other examples where we found that one of the quantities being compared was 1 unit?

• Answers will vary
  • Students might mention the following:

    • In problem 2.2, the cost for 1 small box of cupcakes was $8.50

    In problem 1.2, students choose 1 pepperoni to 2 cheese pizzas

    In Variables and Patterns, the van drove 55 miles in 1 hour

The idea of a unit rate may not have come up explicitly in Investigation 2. (Time) Every rate table has two unit rates. For example, the table in Problem 2.2 showing how amount collected related to number of boxes sold is an example of a rate table. From the table, we can scale down to find the amount collected for 1 box or, not very realistically, the part of a box you get for $1.

The context usually determines which unit rate is the most important. For example, we can consider the context of miles traveled and gallons of fuel used. If you are considering traveling by car, you may be interested in how many miles you can drive using 1 gallon of gas. A car might drive 30 miles using 1 gallon of gas.
